¶

Flow-Based Network Intrusion Detection — Machine Learning Study¶

Author: Luis Rito Filipe¶

June, 2023¶

Summary¶

This study evaluates multiple machine learning models for anomaly detection in network traffic. The objective is to maximize the identification of network attacks while controlling false positives.

The LightGBM algorithm was selected as the best-performing model based on its superior metrics overall and calibration curve, satisfying the defined constraint (Recall ≥ 0.8) and aligning with the objective of maximizing network attacks detection while controlling false positives. The results indicate that network attacks detection probability is driven by a limited number of features, although the importance attributed to these varies across models, highlighting differences in how each algorithm captures complex patterns.

The dataset would benefit from the inclusion of a temporal dimension, enabling the study of network behavior leading up to an attack. This would allow for the investigation of early-stage intrusion detection, rather than classification after the event has already occurred.

About The Study¶

The objective of this study is to compare machine learning algorithms when applied to data analysis to detect network attacks. To achieve this, I will leverage widely used Python libraries specialized in data analysis and machine learning, including tools for data manipulation, visualization, and predictive modeling. The study will progress from exploratory data analysis to more advanced techniques, such as feature engineering and fraud detection modeling, to provide meaningful insights into the characteristics of network attacks detection.

About The Dataset¶

The following study uses a dataset of network intrusion publicly available from Kaggle (see link below). The dataset was produced by Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity.

Dataset link: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/chethuhn/network-intrusion-dataset

In [2]:

pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)

def plot_confusion_matrix(y, y_predict):
    cm = confusion_matrix(y, y_predict)
    plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (5, 3)
    ax = plt.subplot()
    cmap = "Reds"
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap=cmap, ax = ax)
    ax.set_xlabel('Predicted labels')
    ax.set_ylabel('True labels')
    ax.set_title('Confusion Matrix')
    ax.xaxis.set_ticklabels(['Is not attack', 'Is attack'])
    ax.yaxis.set_ticklabels(['Is not attack', 'Is attack'])
    
    plt.show()

Data Wrangling¶

Two datasets with exact the same features, one with DDoS attacks and other with PortScan attacks, were used. After loading and merging the datasets, data must be prepared.

In this study only a subset of features was used ('Flow Bytes/s', 'Flow Packets/s', 'Fwd Packets/s', 'Bwd Packets/s', 'Subflow Fwd Packets', 'Subflow Fwd Bytes', 'Packet Length Std', 'Packet Length Variance', 'Flow Duration', 'Label').

There are 10 features with only one unique value, which imply they have zero variance and cannot help distinguishing Label 0 vs Label 1. Mathematically, for models like logistic regression, the coefficient becomes meaningless and it may even cause numerical instability in some cases. If any of these were included in the subset used, it would also be removed.

Label feature contains three unique values. "BENIGN" will be replaced by 0 (normal), and "DDoS" and "PortScan" by 1 (attack). Label feature was renamed to Class. A space before or after each feature name was removed. A few rows with infinite values were removed.

In [3]:
df1 = pd.read_csv("Friday-WorkingHours-Afternoon-DDos.pcap_ISCX.csv")
df2 = pd.read_csv("Friday-WorkingHours-Afternoon-PortScan.pcap_ISCX.csv")
df = pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0).reset_index(drop=True)
df.info(verbose=False)

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 512212 entries, 0 to 512211
Columns: 79 entries,  Destination Port to  Label
dtypes: float64(24), int64(54), object(1)
memory usage: 308.7+ MB
In [4]:
df.columns = df.columns.str.strip()
df = df[['Flow Bytes/s', 'Flow Packets/s', 'Fwd Packets/s', 'Bwd Packets/s', 'Subflow Fwd Packets', 'Subflow Fwd Bytes', 'Packet Length Std', 'Packet Length Variance', 'Flow Duration', 'Label']]
remove_list = []
for i in range(len(df.columns)):
    if (len(df[df.columns[i]].unique()) == 1):
        remove_list.append(df.columns[i])
df = df.drop(remove_list, axis=1)
df["Label"] = df["Label"].isin(["DDoS", "PortScan"]).astype(int)
df = df.rename(columns={"Label": "Class"})
df.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)
df = df.dropna().reset_index(drop=True)

The following chart shows the correlation between target variable "Class" and the independent variables. The chart reveals that there is no feature with a strong correlation, positive or negative.

In [6]:
def plot_feature_correlation():
    corr = df.corr()["Class"].drop("Class").sort_values(ascending=False)
    sns.set_style("whitegrid")

    full_cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
        "red_white_blue",
        ["#b2182b", "#ffffff", "#08306b"]
    )
    def get_colors(values):
        vmin, vmax = values.min(), values.max()

        if vmin < 0 and vmax > 0:
            norm = TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=vmin, vcenter=0, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = full_cmap
        elif vmin >= 0:
            norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
                "white_blue",
                ["#DEEBF7", "#08306B"]
            )
        else:
            norm = Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)
            cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
                "red_white",
                ["#B2182B", "#DEEBF7"]
            )
        return list(cmap(norm(values)))

    values = corr.values
    colors = get_colors(values)
    
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 3.2))

    sns.barplot(
        x=corr.index,
        y=corr.values,
        hue=corr.index,
        palette=colors,
        legend=False,
        ax=ax
    )

    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=20)
    ax.set_title("Correlation with Class", pad=14)
    ax.set_xlabel(None)
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    ax.axhline(y=0, color="black", linewidth=0.3)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_feature_correlation()

Boxplot analysis highlights that attack traffic (Class 1) generally exhibits fewer outliers across most features, indicating more regular and constrained behavior compared to normal traffic, particularly in features such as “Subflow Fwd Bytes” and “Subflow Fwd Packets”. This supports the idea that normal traffic, associated with browsing, streaming, downloads, and API interactions, is more diverse, whereas attack traffic tends to be automated and repetitive in nature. However, features such as "Packet Length Variance" and "Flow Duration" show notable outliers for attack traffic, suggesting occasional extreme deviations, possibly corresponding to bursts of activity or sustained attack flows. Overall, attack patterns appear more structured but also revealing sporadic high-intensity events.

In [7]:
def plot_box_subplots():
    corr = df.corr(numeric_only=True)["Class"].drop("Class").abs().sort_values(ascending=False)
    features = corr.index
    n_features = len(features)
    cols = 4
    rows = int(np.ceil(n_features / cols))
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(rows, cols, figsize=(18, rows * 2.5))

    axes = axes.flatten()
    df1 = df.copy()
    # for col in features:
    #     if (df1[col] >= 0).all():
    #         df1[col] = np.log1p(df1[col])

    for i, col in enumerate(features):
        df1.boxplot(column=col, by="Class", ax=axes[i], grid=False)
        axes[i].set_title(col)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("")
        
    for j in range(len(features), len(axes)):
        fig.delaxes(axes[j])

    plt.suptitle("Feature Distribution by Class", y=1.001)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_box_subplots()

An initial approach incorporated new features engineered to better capture bytes per packet, flow intensity and packet irregularity. However, this transformation did not improve model performance. This suggests the original features provide the information needed for the machine learning algorithms to capture fundamental patterns, and the additional features engineered with the original features in this dataset do not provide significant additional predictive power.

In [8]:
models = dict()
X = df.drop("Class", axis=1)
Y = df["Class"]
X_train, X_test, Y_train, Y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size=0.33, random_state=42, stratify=Y)

print('X_train.shape=', X_train.shape, 'Y_train.shape=', Y_train.shape)
print('X_test.shape=', X_test.shape, 'Y_test.shape=', Y_test.shape)

X_train.shape= (342910, 9) Y_train.shape= (342910,)
X_test.shape= (168897, 9) Y_test.shape= (168897,)

Logistic Regression

Logistic Regression is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for classification tasks, particularly binary classification problems where the target variable takes values such as 0/1, true/false, or yes/no. In this study, it is applied to predict the "Class" variable, which indicates whether a network flows are associated with attacks or not. Despite its simplicity, Logistic Regression can perform well in high-dimensional datasets and provide a strong baseline for comparison with more complex models. Given the large number of observations relative to the number of features in this dataset, the model is less likely to overfit, although regularization and class imbalance handling remain important considerations. Class weights are incorporated to address the strong imbalance between attacks and non-attacks. The coefficients of the Logistic Regression model provide insight into the direction and relative importance of each feature. Positive values indicate an increase in the likelihood of a network flow being classified as attack, while negative values indicate the opposite. The magnitude of the coefficients reflects the strength of the relationship. Below is the implementation of Logistic Regression with hyperparameter tuning, with emphasis on the adjustment of class weights.

In [9]:
def run_logistic_regression():
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)
    
    parameters = {
            "solver": ['lbfgs'],
            "class_weight": [{0:1, 1:w} for w in range(1, 21)],
            "C": [0.01, 0.1, 1],
            "max_iter": [1000]        
        }

    lr = LogisticRegression()

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=lr,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=10,
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train_scaled, Y_train)
    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
    proba = cv.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)  # fixed threshold
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred),
            "LogLoss": log_loss(Y_test, y_pred),
            "BrierScoreLoss": brier_score_loss(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("log_regression_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("log_regression_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    coefficients = best_model.coef_[0]
    feature_names = X.columns
    df_coef = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": feature_names,
        "Coefficient": coefficients
    })
    df_coef = df_coef.sort_values("Coefficient", ascending=False).round(3).reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "coefficients": df_coef, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

log_regression = run_logistic_regression()
models.update({"Logistic Regression": log_regression})

Fitting 10 folds for each of 10 candidates, totalling 100 fits

In [13]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, log_regression["y_proba"] >= log_regression["metrics"][log_regression["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

Random Forest

Random Forest is often expected to outperform Logistic Regression in network intrusion tasks, as it can capture nonlinear patterns, interactions between features, and is generally more robust regarding noise. The Random Forest hyperparameter search was optimized to reduce computational cost/time by limiting tree complexity and reducing cross-validation folds, while preserving model comparability across experiments.

In [14]:
def run_random_forest():
    parameters = {
            "class_weight": [{0:1, 1:w} for w in range(10, 16)],
            "n_estimators": [200],
            "max_depth": [10],
            "min_samples_leaf": [5],
            "max_features": ["sqrt"]
        }

    rf = RandomForestClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=rf,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=6,             # random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="recall",
        cv=StratifiedKFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=42),                  # cross-validation, 3 or StratifiedKFold(n_splits=2)
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred),
            "LogLoss": log_loss(Y_test, y_pred),
            "BrierScoreLoss": brier_score_loss(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("random_forest_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("random_forest_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    importances = best_model.feature_importances_
    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": importances
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round(3).reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

random_forest = run_random_forest()
models.update({"Random Forest": random_forest})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 6 candidates, totalling 18 fits

The following table shows the computed feature importance ordered from the highest to the lowest.

In [16]:
random_forest["importances"]
Out[16]:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Subflow Fwd Packets Packet Length Std Packet Length Variance Flow Bytes/s Bwd Packets/s Flow Duration Fwd Packets/s Flow Packets/s
Importance 0.439 0.158 0.095 0.086 0.07 0.052 0.046 0.034 0.02

Interestingly, there is a noticeable difference in the importance attributed to features "Subflow Fwd Bytes" and "Flow Packets/s" by each algorithm. "Subflow Fwd Bytes" measures how much forward data is transmitted within segments of a flow, providing insight into how traffic volume is distributed rather than just the total amount. A subflow is a segment of a larger flow. Attack flows (Class 1) tend to send less data forward per segment. This feature may reveal important patterns that can be captured by tree models such as Random Forest.

A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.

In [17]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, random_forest["y_proba"] >= random_forest["metrics"][random_forest["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

XGBoost

XGBoost is expected to show similar metrics compared to Random Forest in this task. Reducing the subsample and colsample_bytree (min ≥ 0.7) from default values (1.0) introduces randomness, reduces overfitting, improves generalization, forces feature diversity and reduces dominance of strong features. Shares some similarities with Random Forest. But while the later builds trees during training and outputs the average of the predictions from individual trees, XGBoost builds new trees sequentially and each new tree corrects errors from the previous tree, improving model's predictions.

In [18]:
def run_xgboost():
    # Note: learning_rate -> estabilidade [0.02-0.05], max_depth -> complexidade [6-12], scale_pos_weight é o parâmetro mais importante para um recall elevado a probabilidades mais elevadas (thresholds)
    scale_pos_weight = len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(0)]) / len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(1)])

    parameters = {
        "n_estimators": [200, 300, 400],
        "max_depth": [8, 9, 10, 11, 12],
        "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.02, 0.05],
        "subsample": [0.7, 0.8, 1.0],
        "colsample_bytree": [0.7, 0.8, 1.0],
        "scale_pos_weight": [scale_pos_weight*0.5, scale_pos_weight, scale_pos_weight*1.5]
    }

    xgb = XGBClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1, eval_metric="logloss" )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=xgb,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=20,             # 20 random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="recall",
        cv=3,                  # cross-validation
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred),
            "LogLoss": log_loss(Y_test, y_pred),
            "BrierScoreLoss": brier_score_loss(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("xgboost_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("xgboost_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    importances = best_model.get_booster().get_score(importance_type="gain")
    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns
    })
    df_importances["Importance"] = df_importances["Feature"].map(importances).fillna(0)
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

xgboost = run_xgboost()
models.update({"XGBoost": xgboost})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 20 candidates, totalling 60 fits
In [19]:

In [21]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, xgboost["y_proba"] >= xgboost["metrics"][xgboost["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0])

LightGBM

LightGBM is a high-performance gradient boosting framework built on decision tree algorithms. It is widely used for ranking, classification, and many other machine learning tasks. Works particularly well with large datasets and a large number of independent variables, optimizing heavily for speed and memory efficiency. LightGBM grows trees leaf-wise, evaluates all leaves and expands the single leaf that reduces the loss the most. It uses histogram-based algorithms to group continuous feature values into discrete bins. This drastically reduces memory usage and speeds up training. It can handle categorical features directly without requiring to manually apply encoding first. Because leaf-wise growth is quite aggressive, LightGBM can easily overfit, especially on smaller datasets. To prevent this, careful tuning of hyperparameters like max_depth, num_leaves, and min_data_in_leaf is strictly required.

In [22]:
def run_light_gbm():
    scale_pos_weight = len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(0)]) / len(Y_train[Y_train.eq(1)])

    parameters = {
        "n_estimators": [200, 300, 400],
        "max_depth": [-1], # -1 = sem restrições
        "num_leaves": [31, 63, 127],
        "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.02, 0.05],
        "scale_pos_weight": [scale_pos_weight*0.5, scale_pos_weight, scale_pos_weight*1.5]
    }

    lgbm = LGBMClassifier( random_state=42, n_jobs=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=lgbm,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=20,             # 20 random combinations
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=3,                  # cross-validation
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42,
        n_jobs=-1
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred),
            "LogLoss": log_loss(Y_test, y_pred),
            "BrierScoreLoss": brier_score_loss(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("light_gbm_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("light_gbm_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": best_model.booster_.feature_importance(importance_type="gain")
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

light_gbm = run_light_gbm()
models.update({"LightGBM": light_gbm})

CatBoost

CatBoost is a high-performance gradient boosting framework built on decision tree algorithms. It is widely used for classification, regression, and ranking tasks, particularly on tabular data. CatBoost introduces ordered boosting, a technique designed to reduce overfitting by preventing information leakage during training. It also uses symmetric trees, where the same splitting condition is applied across each level, improving prediction speed and model stability. One of its main advantages is the ability to handle categorical features directly without requiring extensive preprocessing. CatBoost is generally robust to hyperparameter choices and requires less tuning compared to other gradient boosting frameworks. However, it can be slower to train than LightGBM, especially on very large datasets.

Although CatBoost provides a large number of configurable hyperparameters, it typically requires less manual tuning than frameworks such as LightGBM or XGBoost, due to its robust default settings and built-in mechanisms for handling categorical features and reducing overfitting.

In [26]:
def run_cat_boost():

    parameters = {
        "class_weights": [{0:1, 1:w} for w in [1, 2, 3]],
        "iterations": [300],
        "depth": [4, 6, 8],
        "learning_rate": [0.02, 0.05, 0.1],
        "loss_function": ['Logloss'],
        "eval_metric": ["F1"],
        "verbose": [0]
    }

    catboost = CatBoostClassifier( random_state=42, thread_count=-1 )

    scoring = {
        "recall": "recall",
        "f1": "f1",
        "precision": "precision"
    }

    cv = RandomizedSearchCV(
        estimator=catboost,
        param_distributions=parameters,
        n_iter=10,
        scoring=scoring,
        refit="f1",
        cv=3,
        verbose=1,
        random_state=42
    )

    cv.fit(X_train, Y_train)

    best_model = cv.best_estimator_
        
    proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    results = []
    for t in np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 101):
        y_pred = (proba >= t)
        cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_pred)
        results.append({
            "Threshold": t,
            "PrecisionScore": precision_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "RecallScore": recall_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "F1Score": f1_score(Y_test, y_pred, zero_division=0),
            "TP": cm[1, 1],
            "TN": cm[0, 0],
            "FP": cm[0, 1],
            "FN": cm[1, 0],
            "MCC": matthews_corrcoef(Y_test, y_pred),
            "LogLoss": log_loss(Y_test, y_pred),
            "BrierScoreLoss": brier_score_loss(Y_test, y_pred)
        })

    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(results)
    df_metrics.to_csv("light_gbm_metrics.csv", index=False)
    df_cv_results = pd.DataFrame(cv.cv_results_)
    df_cv_results.to_csv("light_gbm_cv_results.csv", index=False)

    df_importances = pd.DataFrame({
        "Feature": X.columns,
        "Importance": best_model.get_feature_importance()
    })
    df_importances = df_importances.sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).round().reset_index(drop=True).transpose()

    return { "metrics": df_metrics, "best_params": cv.best_params_, "best_model": best_model, "importances": df_importances, "cv_results": df_cv_results, "y_pred": y_pred, "y_proba": proba }

cat_boost = run_cat_boost()
models.update({"CatBoost": cat_boost})

Fitting 3 folds for each of 10 candidates, totalling 30 fits

CatBoost also attributed a similar feature importance compared to Random Forest, XGBoost and LightGBM, with the difference of attributing the least importance to feature "Flow Packets/s". A confusion matrix is shown below for a minimum probability equal to the threshold corresponding to the maximum F1Score for a RecallScore equal or higher than 0.8.

In [29]:
plot_confusion_matrix(Y_test, cat_boost["y_proba"] >= cat_boost["metrics"][cat_boost["metrics"]["RecallScore"] >= 0.8].sort_values("F1Score", ascending=False)["Threshold"].iloc[0] )

Model Comparison and Selection

The next step is to define the desired model outcome. If minimizing false positives is the priority in attacks detection, then configurations with higher precision should be preferred. However, this comes at the cost of increased false negatives, meaning that more attacks may go undetected.

Model selection is performed by first enforcing a minimum Recall of 0.8, ensuring that a sufficient proportion of attacks are detected. Among the models satisfying this constraint, the one with the best metrics overall and calibration curve is selected, as it provides the best balance between precision and recall. This approach aligns model performance with the business requirements of maximizing attacks detection while controlling the number of false positives. This selection process reflects a practical decision framework, where recall acts as a hard requirement and the remaining metrics are used for performance optimization within that constraint.

In [300]:
def model_comparison():
    df_all = []
    
    for name, model in models.items():
        df = model["metrics"].copy()
        df["Model"] = name
        cols = ["Threshold", "PrecisionScore", "RecallScore", "F1Score", "TP", "TN", "FP", "FN", "MCC", "LogLoss", "BrierScoreLoss"]
        df = df[["Model"] + [c for c in cols if c in df.columns]]
        df["ROC_AUC"] = roc_auc_score(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        df["PR_AUC"] = average_precision_score(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        df_all.append(df)
    
    df_all = pd.concat(df_all).reset_index(drop=True)

    # Caso de fraude, mínimo 0.80 >= Recall >= 0.90  -->  escolher o melhor F1Score
    models_best_performance = df_all[df_all["RecallScore"] >= 0.80].sort_values(["F1Score"], ascending=False).drop_duplicates("Model").reset_index(drop=True)

    return { "models_best_performance": models_best_performance, "comparison_across_threshold": df_all }

df_comparison = model_comparison()
In [301]:
df_comparison["models_best_performance"]
Out[301]:
Model Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC LogLoss BrierScoreLoss ROC_AUC PR_AUC
0 CatBoost 0.54 0.999662 0.999451 0.999556 94602 74211 32 52 0.998991 0.017926 0.000497 0.999961 0.999963
1 LightGBM 0.53 0.999672 0.999440 0.999556 94601 74212 31 53 0.998991 0.017926 0.000497 0.999973 0.999978
2 XGBoost 0.75 0.999715 0.999324 0.999519 94590 74216 27 64 0.998907 0.019420 0.000539 0.999968 0.999971
3 Random Forest 0.81 0.999715 0.999187 0.999450 94577 74216 27 77 0.998750 0.022194 0.000616 0.999952 0.999957
4 Logistic Regression 0.92 0.888746 0.981786 0.932953 92930 62610 11633 1724 0.843322 2.850466 0.079084 0.947333 0.942437

Based on the model comparison table above, LightGBM and CatBoost are separated by a very low margin.

Both LightGBM and CatBoost achieved identical F1Scores, indicating equivalent balance between precision and recall. LightGBM achieves a slightly higher precision at a tuned (lower) threshold and produces just one less false positive, which is important in reducing false alarms in intrusion detection systems.

Both models show very high recall. CatBoost reveals one less false negative compared to LightGBM. Both are effective at detecting attacks.

LightGBM slightly outperforms CatBoost in ROC-AUC and PR-AUC. This suggests marginally better ranking and discrimination capability across thresholds. LightGBM provides more consistent behavior across evaluation metrics. A better calibration curve, according to BrierScoreLoss, make it more suitable for real-world deployment.

XGBoost and Random Forest also produced very high evaluation metrics which confirm that ensemble tree-based methods are highly effective to capture non-linear relationships and complex interactions between features. Logistic Regression performed significantly worse, particularly in terms of false positives and overall classification balance. This suggests that the underlying patterns in the data are not linearly separable and require more flexible models capable of modeling non-linear decision boundaries.

As a conclusion, both LightGBM and CatBoost models perform at a very high level with minimal differences in classification metrics. In the context of network intrusion detection, Recall is the most critical metric, as failing to detect an attack has severe consequences. Precision is also important to limit false positives and ensure system usability. LightGBM is therefore the model selected by a small but decisive margin on RecallScore.

In [208]:
def plot_model_comparison():

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))
    
    df_comparison_filtered = df_comparison["comparison_across_threshold"][df_comparison["comparison_across_threshold"]["Model"].isin(list(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4)))]
    data = df_comparison_filtered[~df_comparison_filtered["Threshold"].isin([0.0, 1.0])]
    df_melt = df_comparison["models_best_performance"].head(4).melt(
        id_vars="Model",
        value_vars=["PrecisionScore", "RecallScore", "F1Score"]
    )

    sns.barplot(
        data=df_melt,
        x="Model",
        y="value",
        hue="variable",
        ax=axes[0],
        palette="Reds"  # "Blues", "Reds", "Oranges", "Greens", "Purples", "Greys", "Blues", "deep", "muted", "dark", "colorblind"
    )

    axes[0].set_title("ML Model Performance Comparison at Maximum F1Score for Recall ≥ 0.80", pad=14)
    axes[0].set_ylim(0.999, 1)
    axes[0].set_ylabel("Score (scale magnified)")
    axes[0].set_xlabel("")
    axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=0, pad=10)
    axes[0].legend(loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.12), ncol=3)

    # Precision
    sns.lineplot(
        data=data,
        x="Threshold",
        y="PrecisionScore",
        hue="Model",
        ax=axes[1],
        palette="Set1"
    )

    # Recall
    sns.lineplot(
        data=data,
        x="Threshold",
        y="RecallScore",
        hue="Model",
        ax=axes[1],
        linestyle="--",
        palette="Set1"
    )
    
    axes[1].set_title("Precision & Recall vs Threshold", pad=14)
    axes[1].set_ylabel("Score")

    handles, labels = axes[1].get_legend_handles_labels()
    new_labels = [
        f"{label} {'(P)' if i < len(labels)//2 else '(R)'}"
        for i, label in enumerate(labels)
    ]

    axes[1].legend(handles[:len(data)], new_labels[:len(data)], loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.12), ncol=4)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

plot_model_comparison()

Precision–Recall curves are nearly identical (square-like shape), confirming that both models have very high class separation ability and similar ranking performance. Differences in overall classification performance are negligible and within expected variance.

In [132]:
def model_precision_recall_curve():

    valid_models = set(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4))
    filtered_models = {k: v for k, v in models.items() if k in valid_models}
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4))

    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(filtered_models.items()):
        precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(Y_test, model["y_proba"])
        df = pd.DataFrame({"precision": precision, "recall": recall})
        sns.lineplot(
            data=df,
            x="recall",
            y="precision",
            ax=axes[i]
            #label=name
        )

        axes[i].set_title(f"Precision-Recall Curve: {name}", pad=14)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("Recall")
        axes[i].set_ylabel("Precision")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

model_precision_recall_curve()

The calibration curve exhibits probability quality. Visually, LightGBM calibration curve seems to follow more closely the perfect calibrated line, unlike CatBoost that shows a local miscalibration issue around predicted probability at 0.5, as the observed (true) probability is close to 0. This indicates less reliable probability estimates for CatBoost in that region.

LightGBM demonstrates better flexibility when adjusting decision thresholds. CatBoost appears slightly less stable in probability quality space.

In [149]:
def model_calibration_curve():

    valid_models = set(df_comparison["models_best_performance"]["Model"].head(4))
    filtered_models = {k: v for k, v in models.items() if k in valid_models}
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4.5))

    for i, (name, model) in enumerate(filtered_models.items()):
        bins = 11
        prob_true, prob_pred = calibration_curve(Y_test, model["y_proba"], n_bins=bins)
        df = pd.DataFrame({"prob_true": prob_true, "prob_pred": prob_pred})
        
        # modelo
        sns.lineplot(
            data=df,
            x="prob_pred",
            y="prob_true",
            ax=axes[i],
            marker='o',
            label="Model"
        )

        # Perfeitamente calibrada
        sns.lineplot(
            x=[0, 1],
            y=[0, 1],
            ax=axes[i],
            linestyle='--',
            label="Perfectly calibrated"
        )
        
        axes[i].set_title(f"Calibration Curve: {name}", pad=14)
        axes[i].set_xlabel("Predicted probability")
        axes[i].set_ylabel("True probability")
        axes[i].legend(loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.18), ncol=4)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

model_calibration_curve()

The following plot shows that attacks detection is mainly driven by two features. Feature "Subflow Fwd Bytes" has the strongest influence, with high concentration of lower values increasing the probability of attacks, and higher values decreasing the probability of attacks. This is followed by features "Packet Length Variance" and "Bwd Packets/s", where higher values are more strongly associated with probability of attacks, while a high concentration of lower values leads to a lower probability of attacks.

In [310]:
def features_impact(model):
    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message="CatBoost binary classifier with TreeExplainer shap values output has changed")
    explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
    shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)
    return shap_values

shap.summary_plot(features_impact(cat_boost["best_model"]), X_test, plot_size=(6, 7))

The SHAP dependence plot shows how the contribution of feature "Subflow Fwd Bytes" to the model prediction varies across its value range, while coloring by "Flow Bytes/s", the feature with the strongest interaction with "Subflow Fwd Bytes". Different values of "Flow Bytes/s" are associated with different SHAP effects of "Subflow Fwd Bytes": when "Flow Bytes/s" is lower (blue points), "Subflow Fwd Bytes" tends to have a mixed contribution to SHAP values (stronger negative or positive contribution to the prediction) when its values are significantly low, whereas the majority of higher values of "Flow Bytes/s" (red points) are more associated with negative SHAP contributions from "Subflow Fwd Bytes". This suggests a strong interaction between "Subflow Fwd Bytes" and "Flow Bytes/s", meaning the effect of "Subflow Fwd Bytes" on the model output is conditional on the value of "Flow Bytes/s".

In [303]:
shap.dependence_plot("Subflow Fwd Bytes", features_impact(cat_boost["best_model"]), X_test)

Conclusion

The dataset contains features that already encode attack behavior very explicitly, which leads to near-perfect class separation across multiple models. This reduces the difficulty of the task and limits its usefulness for evaluating realistic intrusion detection scenarios, particularly for early-stage detection.

In [ ]:
"python -m nbconvert --to html --no-input Network_intrusion.ipynb"
